p = (0.94, 0.78, f)
p′ = (−0.94, −0.78, −f),   x=fX/Z, y=fY/Z
The Physical Pinhole Camera
Use sliders to move the 3D point and focal length.
⚠ Figure failed to load

elements: pinhole point at the origin (0,0,0) labeled 'Pinhole'
world coordinate axes: X (lateral), Y (vertical), Z (optical axis)
3D world point P at coordinates (X, Y, Z)
projection plane (real sensor) at z = -f, colored dark gray with dashed border
virtual camera plane at z = +f, colored light gray with solid border
inverted projected point p' on the real projection plane at (-f*X/Z, -f*Y/Z, -f)
upright projected point p on the virtual camera plane at (f*X/Z, f*Y/Z, f)
light ray extending from world point P, through the pinhole, to the inverted point p'
virtual projection ray from the pinhole to the upright point p
local 2D coordinate axes (x, y) on the virtual camera plane (green, centered)
local 2D coordinate axes (x, y) on the real projection plane (green, inverted)
pixel coordinate axes (n, m) at the bottom-left corner of the virtual camera plane (blue)
dimension markers for focal length f from pinhole to both planes (red double-headed arrows)
dashed perpendicular projection guides from P, p, and p' to their respective optical axes and baselines

interactions.label: World X
interactions.teaches: Moving the world point sideways causes linear displacement on both planes; notice how the real plane projection moves in the opposite direction (inverted) while the virtual plane moves in the same direction.

interactions.label: World Y
interactions.teaches: Changing the height of the world point demonstrates vertical inversion on the real physical sensor versus the upright orientation on the virtual plane.

interactions.label: Depth Z
interactions.teaches: Increasing depth decreases the size of the projection on both planes according to the 1/Z scaling rule, representing perspective foreshortening.

interactions.label: Focal Length (f)
interactions.teaches: Increasing the focal length pushes the projection planes further from the pinhole, enlarging (zooming) the projected image without changing the physical 3D world.

interactions.label: Show Real Sensor Plane
interactions.teaches: Toggles the visibility of the real inverted projection plane located behind the pinhole to clarify physical camera mechanics.

interactions.label: Show Pixel Grid (n, m)
interactions.teaches: Overlays the image/pixel coordinate grid (n, m) with its origin at the corner, showing how camera coordinates (x, y) map to discrete pixel arrays.

demo_steps.title: The Physical Pinhole Camera
demo_steps.narration: In a physical pinhole camera, light rays from a 3D object point P cross through the pinhole (origin) and hit the real projection sensor at depth -f. Because the rays cross, the resulting image is inverted both horizontally and vertically (point p').

demo_steps.title: The Convenient Virtual Plane
demo_steps.narration: To simplify coordinate systems, computer vision uses a mathematically equivalent 'virtual camera plane' placed in front of the pinhole at +f. The projection point p on this plane has the exact same coordinate signs as the 3D point P, making the math upright and intuitive.

demo_steps.title: Depth and Perspective Scaling
demo_steps.narration: Watch what happens as we push the world point P further away to depth Z=14. The similarity of triangles forces the projected point p closer to the optical axis (0,0). This is perspective projection: depth division scales dimensions by f/Z.

demo_steps.title: Focal Length as Zoom
demo_steps.narration: Let's increase the focal length f to 4.5. Both projection planes slide further away from the pinhole. The projection point p expands outward away from the center. This is zooming: a larger focal length magnifies the projection of the same 3D scene.

demo_steps.title: Mapping to Pixel Coordinates
demo_steps.narration: Finally, enable the pixel grid (n, m). While physical camera coordinates (x, y) are centered at (0,0) with continuous metric units, actual digital images use pixel indices (n, m) starting from the bottom-left (or top-left) corner. This translation and scaling is modeled by an affine camera calibration matrix.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Perspective view positioned slightly above and to the right of the Z-axis (e.g., eye: [12, 6, 12], target: [0, 0, 2]) to clearly capture both the real projection plane on the left, the pinhole at center, and the virtual plane/world points on the right.
interactionPlan.notes: Render the light rays as distinct colors: a solid bright ray from P to the pinhole, continuing as a dashed ray to the real sensor, and a different colored ray reaching the virtual plane. Make sure the pixel grid on the virtual plane correctly shifts its origin coordinates to the bottom-left corner when the 'showPixelGrid' toggle is active, drawing blue axes labeled 'n' and 'm' matching diagram (b).

attempts.html:
The Physical Pinhole Camera
Use sliders to move the 3D point and focal length.
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The 3D model is extremely small and zoomed out compared to the original figure, leaving too much empty space.
The Z-axis points towards the viewer/left instead of horizontally to the right as in the source figure.
Multiple labels overlap severely in the default view (e.g., 'World coordinates' and 'Camera coordinates').
Part (b) of the original figure (the 2D image coordinate layout) is not cleanly separated, causing clutter when its elements are overlaid directly on the 3D virtual plane.
evaluation.notes: The mathematical implementation of the pinhole camera and the interactive step-by-step UI are excellent, but the default camera framing makes the model tiny, and overlapping labels degrade readability.
action_items: Adjust the default camera position and zoom to make the 3D figure fill the canvas, aligning the viewpoint so the Z-axis points to the right.
Resolve label overlaps by shifting the 3D positions or vertical offsets of 'World coordinates', 'Camera coordinates', and 'Pinhole'.
Increase the thickness of the axes, lines, and plane borders to make them more prominent and legible.
Refine the 'Show Pixel Grid' state to better replicate the clean 2D layout of Part (b), perhaps by auto-rotating the camera to face the virtual plane directly when active.

feedback.rationale: The conceptual model and interactive structure are excellent (scoring 5), but the execution needs concrete visual fixes including camera framing, axis reorientation, and label overlap resolution.
action_items: Adjust the default camera position and zoom to make the 3D figure fill the canvas, aligning the viewpoint so the Z-axis points to the right.
Resolve label overlaps by shifting the 3D positions or vertical offsets of 'World coordinates', 'Camera coordinates', and 'Pinhole'.
Increase the thickness of the axes, lines, and plane borders to make them more prominent and legible.
Refine the 'Show Pixel Grid' state to better replicate the clean 2D layout of Part (b), perhaps by auto-rotating the camera to face the virtual plane directly when active.

attempts.html:
p = (0.94, 0.78, f),   p′ = (−x, −y, −f)
The Physical Pinhole Camera
Use sliders to move the 3D point and focal length.
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The left-to-right layout is completely reversed: the projection plane is on the right and the virtual camera plane is on the left, whereas the original textbook has the projection plane on the far left and the virtual plane to the right of the pinhole.
The Z-axis points to the left/bottom-left instead of pointing to the right as in the textbook.
The coordinate axes on the virtual and projection planes have different directions compared to the reference.
The label locations are cluttered and somewhat overlapping due to the reversed camera perspective.
evaluation.notes: Excellent interactive controls and step-by-step demonstrations, but the camera viewpoint is reversed, placing the projection plane on the right and virtual plane on the left, which directly contradicts the textbook layout.
action_items: Reposition the camera (adjust initial camera.position and controls.target) so that the Z-axis points to the right, matching the textbook's left-to-right layout (Projection Plane -> Pinhole -> Virtual Plane -> Point P).
Adjust the coordinate axes of both planes to match the direction vectors shown in the textbook (e.g., projection plane axes pointing down and left).
Refine label offsets once the camera viewpoint is corrected to prevent overlapping and improve readability.
Ensure the green/blue coordinate arrows on the planes match the 2D plane orientations accurately.

feedback.rationale: The concept and interactivity are highly accurate, but the camera viewpoint and coordinate axis directions are reversed, which is a styling and positioning detail best addressed by refining the generation.
action_items: Reposition the camera (adjust initial camera.position and controls.target) so that the Z-axis points to the right, matching the textbook's left-to-right layout (Projection Plane -> Pinhole -> Virtual Plane -> Point P).
Adjust the coordinate axes of both planes to match the direction vectors shown in the textbook (e.g., projection plane axes pointing down and left).
Refine label offsets once the camera viewpoint is corrected to prevent overlapping and improve readability.
Ensure the green/blue coordinate arrows on the planes match the 2D plane orientations accurately.
actionItems: Reposition the camera (adjust initial camera.position and controls.target) so that the Z-axis points to the right, matching the textbook's left-to-right layout (Projection Plane -> Pinhole -> Virtual Plane -> Point P).

Refine label offsets once the camera viewpoint is corrected to prevent overlapping and improve readability.
Ensure the green/blue coordinate arrows on the planes match the 2D plane orientations accurately.

p = (0.94, 0.78, f)

The Physical Pinhole Camera

⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The projected points P, p, and p' appear as solid black spheres in the rendering, whereas they should be light blue (cyan) with a dark outline as in the original.
The green coordinate axes on the projection plane and virtual camera plane have slightly different directions/placements compared to the original.
The red double-headed arrows indicating focal length 'f' are styled differently and are less prominent than the original's red arrows.
Some labels (like 'Camera coordinates (x,y)') are slightly overlapping or crowded near the axes.
evaluation.notes: Excellent interactive implementation of the pinhole camera concept with step-by-step guidance, though the point colors rendering as black and minor label crowding prevent a perfect score.
action_items: Change the inner color of the spheres (P, p, p') to a bright cyan/light blue (#55dfff) and reduce the outer black outline sphere radius slightly to ensure the color is clearly visible.
Use Georgia or serif fonts for math labels such as 'P', 'p', 'p′', and 'f' to match the textbook style.
Slightly adjust the offset of 'Camera coordinates (x,y)' and 'World coordinates' to prevent overlap with the axes.
Ensure the green camera coordinate axes on the virtual plane align perfectly with the x and y projection directions shown in the original diagram.

feedback.rationale: The conceptual planning and interactivity are excellent, but minor implementation details like point colors, label fonts, and layout overlap need refinement to achieve full faithfulness to the original.
action_items: Change the inner color of the spheres (P, p, p') to a bright cyan/light blue (#55dfff) and reduce the outer black outline sphere radius slightly to ensure the color is clearly visible.

Ensure the green camera coordinate axes on the virtual plane align perfectly with the x and y projection directions shown in the original diagram.
